artworksPage.title: Buy Artworks
artworksPage.seoTitle: Buy Original Korean Art — {artworkCount} Paintings, Prints, Photography & Sculpture | SAF Online
artworksPage.metaDescription: Browse {artworkCount} original Korean artworks by {artistCount} artists. Proceeds fund {loanCount} low-interest loans (95% repayment). Paintings, prints, photography, sculpture. Free shipping.
artworksPage.heroDescription: Original works from {artistCount} Korean artists at accessible prices. Free shipping, 7-day returns.
artworksPage.shareTitle: 1 artwork purchase = 1 artist low-interest loan — {artworkCount} original Korean artworks | SAF Online
artworksPage.shareDescription: {artistCount} Korean artists' originals · 1 purchase = 1 mutual-aid loan (95% repayment rate). Paintings, prints, photography, sculpture · free shipping.
artworksPage.introHeading: Buy Original Korean Art — Direct from the Gallery
artworksPage.introParagraph1: SAF Online is a direct-from-gallery marketplace where you can purchase original artworks from {artistCount} Korean artists — paintings, prints, photography, and sculpture. Every piece is authenticated through gallery review, with the artist's signature and certificate of authenticity included.
artworksPage.introParagraph2: Sales proceeds become a mutual aid fund that has provided {loanCount} low-interest loans (95% repayment rate) to fellow artists facing financial discrimination. Buying a painting becomes an act of solidarity that sustains Korea's art ecosystem.
artworksPage.categoryGuideIntro: Explore Korean original art by genre — paintings, prints, photography, sculpture. Each genre has its own price range and aesthetic.
artworksPage.categoryPaintingDescription: One-of-a-kind oil, acrylic, and watercolor works on canvas. Each painting is unique — a single piece that exists nowhere else, carrying the direct touch of the Korean contemporary artist.
artworksPage.categoryPrintDescription: Limited-edition prints and original drawings. More accessible price points than paintings, making them ideal for first-time collectors.
artworksPage.categoryPhotoDescription: Signature photographic works from Korean photographers, available as limited-edition fine art prints with artist signature and edition number.
artworksPage.categorySculptureDescription: Three-dimensional sculptures and ceramic/craft originals that bring tactile presence to any space — equally suited for interior design and serious collection.
artworksPage.faqA1: Every piece is an original submitted directly by the artist and authenticated through gallery review. A signed certificate of authenticity is included; limited-edition works ship with edition numbers.
artworksPage.faqA2: Domestic shipping is free. After payment, we inspect the work and pack it safely. Works that need custom framing may take 1-2 weeks because of the framing process, while works that do not need framing usually arrive within 3-4 days. Canvas and framed works receive double-layered protective packaging.
artworksPage.faqQ3: Can I return artwork I've bought?
artworksPage.faqA3: Yes — within 7 days of receipt for any reason (round-trip shipping at buyer's expense). Damaged works are exchanged or refunded at no cost.
artworksPage.faqA4: Artists set their own prices, and proceeds go directly to the artist without additional gallery commission. Prices range from ₩100,000 to ₩50,000,000.
artworksPage.faqQ5: How does buying art help other artists?
artworksPage.faqA5: Sales proceeds accumulate as a mutual aid fund, providing low-interest loans to artists shut out by traditional finance. To date, {loanCount} loans have been issued with a 95% repayment rate.
artworksPage.faqQ6: I'm new to collecting — where should I start?
artworksPage.faqA6: Set a budget first, then narrow down to a genre you connect with. Prints and drawings in the ₩300K–1M range are a comfortable entry point. Each artwork page includes the artist's profile and exhibition history.

support.title: Art protects art
support.stat0Label: artists are shut out by banks
support.stat2Label: repayment rate — trust comes full circle
support.soldCount: {count} artworks sold, each becoming a seed of solidarity
support.body:
Until the next exhibition, the next performance.
For artists, income gaps are an unavoidable reality.

For fellow artists forced into predatory loans just to afford paint, canvas, and studio rent, proceeds from this artwork become the Seed Fund — extending a fair hand at fair rates.
support.footer: One artwork becomes the oxygen that keeps a fellow artist creating.

galleryCampaign.heading: Every purchase makes a difference
galleryCampaign.descriptionLine1: These artists are building a future together with fellow artists.
galleryCampaign.descriptionLine2: Join the mutual aid fund through artwork purchases or co-op membership.

ohYoon.metaTitle: Oh Yoon, his prints and artworks, and a petition for estate rights | SAF
ohYoon.metaDescription: Learn about Korean Minjung artist Oh Yoon, his prints and artworks, and the rights issue faced by his estate. A citizens' petition for the proper recovery of Oh Yoon's art.
ohYoon.breadcrumb: Oh Yoon Guui-dong Mural Citizens' Petition
ohYoon.heroImageAlt: Oh Yoon's mural — front view of the Guui-dong terracotta human-figure relief
ohYoon.heroTitle:
Save Oh Yoon's mural
from demolition. Bring it home.
ohYoon.heroSubtitle: A citizens' petition to preserve Oh Yoon's 1974 terracotta artwork and restore estate rights
ohYoon.crisis: A wall carved in 1974 — about to vanish, fifty years on.
ohYoon.storyHeading: A leading Korean modern artist, carved at age 28
ohYoon.muralAltFront: Oh Yoon, 1974 — terracotta mural, front (figural relief)
ohYoon.muralAltDetail: Oh Yoon, 1974 — terracotta mural, figural relief detail
ohYoon.muralAltBack: Oh Yoon, 1974 — terracotta mural, reverse face (abstract relief)
ohYoon.muralCaption: On site in Guui-dong, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul. A double-sided terracotta relief — the figural face (left and center) and the abstract face (right).
ohYoon.storyQuote: Art should be shared by everyone.
ohYoon.storyPortraitCaption: A master of Korean people's art
ohYoon.storyP1: Oh Yoon (1946–1986). In a life of just forty years he left some 400 works across painting, printmaking, and sculpture. He was posthumously awarded the Order of Cultural Merit (Jade Crown) in 2005, and a major retrospective followed at the National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art in 2006. The weight of a life given to that work still reaches us today.
ohYoon.storyP2: 1974. He was twenty-eight. Discharged from the army on medical grounds, he was working at brick kilns in Gyeongju and Ilsan — handling clay, sweating beside the laborers. In that very period, Commercial Bank of Korea (today's Woori Bank) commissioned him to carve terracotta reliefs into the inner and outer walls of its Dongdaemun and Guui-dong branches. A young man who was himself a laborer, carving on both sides of a wall the laborers passed every day — a singular early example of Korean public art, made in an era when young artists were rarely entrusted with such work.
ohYoon.storyP3: Even after carving this wall, the medium he devoted himself to most fiercely was woodblock printmaking. The rough laughter of Busan's markets, the sweat of dock workers, the people's vitality that turns crushed han (恨) into the rapture of dance — all of it he carved into wood with the edge of a blade. And those prints, pressed not by machine but by a spoon rubbed against paper, he gave away unsparingly — to the covers of poetry collections, to leaflets at workers' sites, to the illustrations of children's storybooks. ‘Art should be shared by everyone' — exactly as he said above.
ohYoon.threeStepsLead: This spring the building was sold. But three sequential acts have opened a path to preservation.
ohYoon.threeStepsCard1Body: Informed the buyer of the artwork's existence during the sale.
ohYoon.threeStepsCard2Body: Recognised the work's value, contacted the National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art, located the artist's family, and reached out personally.
ohYoon.threeStepsCard3Body: Oh Yoon's family has actively wished for the work to be preserved and transferred to public ownership.
ohYoon.threeStepsBridge: With citizens' hands added now, the work returns to all of us.
ohYoon.statementLine1: We ask Seoul's next mayor and the Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism
ohYoon.statementLine3: the safe removal, preservation, and transfer of Oh Yoon's 1974 Guui-dong mural.
ohYoon.participationCard1Body: Just your name, email, and phone — that's all it takes.
ohYoon.participationCard2Title: 2. Join as a citizen committee member (optional)
ohYoon.participationCard2Body: If you would like to put your name forward more firmly, join the citizen committee. Your name appears on the founding declaration.
ohYoon.participationCard2Cta: ↓ Check the committee box on the form
ohYoon.participationCard3Body: Send this page to just five people. If they each pass it to five more, we reach 10,000 names together.
ohYoon.urgencyLead: From this August, the building will be torn down and replaced. In that process, the work disappears.
ohYoon.urgencyBullet1: 2026 marks the 40th anniversary of Oh Yoon's passing.
ohYoon.urgencyBullet2: 2026 is seeing Seoul's next mayor and new national cultural policy decided at once.
ohYoon.urgencyBullet3: The deadline for safe removal is fixed: by early August at the latest.
ohYoon.urgencyTail: If citizens' names are not gathered now, a work that endured fifty years is finally lost.
ohYoon.proponentsBody1: This petition is a citizens' movement begun by the Korea Smart Cooperative (an artists' cooperative). Hand in hand with Oh Yoon's family, the cooperative has taken on the work's conservation removal and public transfer, and has signed an artwork removal agreement with the buyer.
ohYoon.proponentsBody2: The Oh Yoon Artwork Preservation Committee will be founded in early May, gathering co-chairs and members from the art world, civil society, and academia.
ohYoon.proponentsCommitteeNote: The list of co-chairs will be posted here as soon as it is confirmed.
ohYoon.proponentsExhibitionLine: Discover more of Oh Yoon's work in the <link>Oh Yoon 40th Anniversary Special Exhibition</link>.
ohYoon.exhibitionCardSubtitle: His art, and the future of fellow artists
ohYoon.exhibitionCardBody: Meet the spirit of an era carved into roughly 100 woodblock prints. Every purchase becomes a mutual aid loan fund — carrying the artist's belief into today's living artists.
ohYoon.exploreStoriesLabel: Read stories on artist financial equity
ohYoon.faqQ1: Why Seoul's next mayor and the Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism?
ohYoon.faqA1: 2026 is the year Seoul's mayor is decided. We gather citizens' will before candidate registration so the incoming mayor can receive this work as an asset for the citizens of Seoul. Additionally, Oh Yoon's 1974 work is a landmark in modern Korean art history. The incoming Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism holds policy responsibility for preserving works of this cultural and historical value.
ohYoon.faqA2: Used only for petition tally, transmission to the next Seoul mayor and the Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism, and progress updates. Retention and disposal policies are detailed in the consent statement at signing.
ohYoon.faqA3: We will respectfully send progress updates to the email you provide.
ohYoon.proposalDocTitle: Citizens' Proposal — Oh Yoon's Guui-dong Mural
ohYoon.proposalSection1Body1: Oh Yoon (1946–1986) is one of the leading figures of Korean modern art. He was posthumously awarded the Order of Cultural Merit (Jade Crown, 2005), and a major retrospective was held at the National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art (MMCA) in 2006. In 1974, at age 28, he carved a double-sided terracotta mural into a building in Guui-dong, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul. The work was commissioned by Commercial Bank of Korea — a rare instance of public art entrusted to a young artist in an era when such commissions were uncommon.
ohYoon.proposalSection1Body2: This spring the building was sold. Unless the work is safely removed and relocated by early August 2026 at the latest, it faces demolition.
ohYoon.proposalSection2Body1: (a) Art-historical value — Oh Yoon is regarded as one of the defining figures of Korean people's art (minjung misul), an artist who recorded the wounds of his era with the blade of a woodcut. Among his roughly 400 works across painting, printmaking, and sculpture, the 1974 Guui-dong mural — a double-sided relief carved at age 28 — is a rare early example of Korean public art, made in an era when young artists were seldom commissioned for public work.
ohYoon.proposalSection2Body2: (b) Civic memory — a work that stood in one place for 50 years has become both a neighborhood's time and a civic asset.
ohYoon.proposalSection2Body3: (c) The moment of 2026 — coinciding with the 40th anniversary of Oh Yoon's passing. If the work is lost now, it can never be returned to citizens' hands.
ohYoon.proposalSection3Step1: (1) Woori Bank informed the buyer of the work's existence during the sale process.
ohYoon.proposalSection3Step2: (2) The buyer, recognizing the work's value, contacted the MMCA directly and reached out to Oh Yoon's family.
ohYoon.proposalSection3Step3: (3) The family has actively sought the work's preservation and public transfer.
ohYoon.proposalSection3Summary: In consultation with the family, the Korea Smart Cooperative has taken on the conservation removal and public transfer effort, and has signed an artwork removal agreement with the buyer.
ohYoon.proposalSection4Body: We respectfully petition Seoul's next mayor and the Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism, in the name of citizens, to take responsibility for the safe removal, conservation, and public transfer of Oh Yoon's 1974 Guui-dong mural.
ohYoon.saffestHeading: Why SAF stands with this petition
ohYoon.saffestBody: SAF (Seed Art Festival) 2026 is a mutual-aid platform easing financial discrimination against Korean artists. The conditions a living artist deserves (mutual-aid loans), and the place a departed artist's work deserves to reach (the Oh Yoon petition) — different in kind, but the same intent: restoring the social standing of Korean artists and the public value of their work.
ohYoon.closingLine1: Oh Yoon's 1974 work faces demolition after 50 years.
ohYoon.closingLine2: Now let us protect it together.
ohYoon.closingLine3: Bring Oh Yoon's work into citizens' hands.
ohYoon.tributeTitle: Oh Yoon's 40th Anniversary — Meeting His Work Again
ohYoon.tributeLead: Oh Yoon (吳潤, 1946–1986), the artist who carved the very mural this petition seeks to save in 1974. He carved the shadow of his era and the cheers of the public square onto a single blade, and left us in the summer of his fortieth year.
ohYoon.tributeBody1: The 100 or so woodblock prints Oh Yoon left behind remain a spiritual map of Korean people's art in the 1980s, and a body of imagery that still touches the public closer than any other. In his hands, the carving knife was not a tool but a question put to its time; a single print was not merely a picture but the faces of countless unnamed people. Forty years after his passing, his cuts still ask us — whose faces are we keeping unforgotten?
ohYoon.tributeBody2: To mark Oh Yoon's 40th anniversary, SAF has prepared a special exhibition that opens his world of work to citizens once more. From the 1985 Song of the Blade series to the 1996 posthumous print collection, scattered plates are gathered in one place — and you can follow them slowly, with curated text alongside each work, tracing what the artist was asking and what he meant to inscribe. It is a place where you can meet, work by work, the full range of Oh Yoon that a single Guui-dong mural could never tell on its own.
ohYoon.tributeBody3: When those who have added their names to this petition also meet the artist's broader world of work, this movement becomes more than protecting a single wall — it becomes the citizens' way of remembering an artist's era together. Welcoming a single work into your home is itself another way of carrying the departed artist's beliefs forward to the living artists of today: proceeds from each sale fund mutual-aid loans for working artists, rebuilding the place his lifelong colleagues deserve.
ohYoon.tributeClosingNote: He left in the summer of his fortieth year — and the faces he carved are still here with us today.
ohYoon.missionBridgeHeading: Standing together was Oh Yoon's way
ohYoon.missionBridgeBody: SAF is a mutual-aid fund built by artists, for artists facing financial discrimination. Artwork sales fund low-interest loans that reach working Korean artists directly. Becoming a member makes you part of that solidarity.
ohYoon.formComingSoon: The signing form will appear here shortly.
ohYoon.formAgeNotice: Citizens aged 14 and over may sign this petition.
ohYoon.closedBody: Heartfelt thanks to the {total} who lent their names. The will of these citizens will be respectfully delivered to the next Seoul mayor and the Minister of Culture, Sports and Tourism.
ohYoon.shareTemplatesHeading: When sending to five people, paste this text as-is
ohYoon.shareTemplateKakao: Oh Yoon's 1974 Guui-dong terracotta mural faces demolition this August. We petition Seoul's next mayor and Culture Minister to preserve this two-sided relief by a leading Korean modern artist. Join us with a 30-second signature. → {url}

ohYoonMemorial.metaDescription: Oh Yoon 40th memorial on July 5, 2026. Join the bus from Insadong. First-come, first-served.
ohYoonMemorial.feeNotice: Fee KRW 30,000 per person · One 45-seat bus, first-come first-served
ohYoonMemorial.formEmailHelp: Enter to receive a receipt and details (optional)
ohYoonMemorial.formBoardingLabel: I agree to board next to Insadong Suun Hall at 09:30
ohYoonMemorial.formPrivacyLabel: I agree to the collection and use of personal information (required)
ohYoonMemorial.paymentTransferHelp: Your seat is reserved immediately and we will send you the deposit account. Your spot is confirmed once we verify the deposit.
ohYoonMemorial.depositLead: Please transfer the fee to the account below. Your seat is reserved, and your spot is confirmed once we verify the deposit.
ohYoonMemorial.depositNotice: Please make the depositor name match the applicant name. We will send confirmation by KakaoTalk (and email).
ohYoonMemorial.waitlistBody: If a seat opens up, we will contact you in order for payment.
ohYoonMemorial.successBody: We have sent confirmation via KakaoTalk (and email).
ohYoonMemorial.errorGeneric: Something went wrong. Please try again in a moment.
ohYoonMemorial.faqQ1: How do I pay the fee, and what does it cover?
ohYoonMemorial.faqA1: The fee of KRW 30,000 per person covers the round-trip chartered bus, lunch at Insadong Pungnyu-sarang after the ceremony, and the running of the memorial. When you submit the form, a card payment window (Toss Payments) opens; your seat is confirmed once payment completes. We do not accept bank transfers or on-site payment.
ohYoonMemorial.faqA2: Yes. Enter the total number of attendees including yourself under “Number of attendees.” The fee is summed accordingly and paid at once, and seats are secured for that many people. We do not collect companions’ names separately.
ohYoonMemorial.faqQ3: What happens when it is full?
ohYoonMemorial.faqA3: Seats fill first-come on a single 45-seat bus (44 seats). After that we take waitlist registrations. If a seat opens through a cancellation, we contact waitlisted applicants in order with a payment link, and your place is confirmed once you pay. No fee is charged at the waitlist stage.

schedule.depart: Depart next to Insadong Suun Hall

ohYoonMemorial.storyQuote: Art must be shared by many
ohYoonMemorial.storyP1: Born the son of the novelist Oh Young-su, Oh Yoon chose to record his era with the tip of a knife rather than with words. He took part in founding Reality and Utterance (1979) and stood at the heart of Korea’s Minjung art movement; the woodblock prints he carved passed hand to hand through campuses, factory walls, and market alleys.
ohYoonMemorial.storyP2: The woodblock print he chose was a resolve that could not be undone once the knife met the wood — and the most democratic vessel of all, one he could rub out by the tens of thousands with a spoon, without even a press, and give away. The hearty laughter of a Busan market, the sweat of Guro factory workers, buried sorrow released as ecstatic spirit — he kept all of it alive within those rough cuts.
ohYoonMemorial.storyP3: In an age when censorship silenced the direct voice, he entered the shaman’s rite, carving sword dances and goblins to resist from the deepest place. Then in July 1986, not long after his first solo exhibition, he passed away at forty. The works of that brief life still touch the most aching places of our time, forty years on.
ohYoonMemorial.portraitAlt: Portrait of the artist Oh Yoon (1946–1986)
ohYoonMemorial.portraitCaption: Oh Yoon (1946–1986) · Master of Korean Minjung art
ohYoonMemorial.muralCaption: A double-sided terracotta relief that the 28-year-old Oh Yoon carved into the walls of a commercial bank branch in Guui-dong — a young laborer carving where laborers passed every day.
ohYoonMemorial.muralAlt1: Oh Yoon 1974 Guui-dong terracotta mural — figural relief, front
ohYoonMemorial.muralAlt2: Oh Yoon 1974 Guui-dong terracotta mural — figural relief detail
ohYoonMemorial.muralAlt3: Oh Yoon 1974 Guui-dong terracotta mural — abstract relief side
ohYoonMemorial.posterAlt: Oh Yoon 40th memorial poster — departs Insadong on Sun, July 5, 2026, fee KRW 30,000
ohYoonMemorial.applyLead: Choose the number of attendees below and pay the fee to complete registration.
ohYoonMemorial.meaningTitle:
Forty summers since
he laid down the knife
ohYoonMemorial.meaningP1: To remember Oh Yoon is more than to recall an artist. His belief that “art must be shared by many” lives on today — where the proceeds of a single work become another struggling artist’s next month, in a place of mutual aid.
ohYoonMemorial.meaningP2: Forty years after the summer of 1986 when he set down his blade, on July 5 his friends, his juniors, and the citizens who loved him gather in Insadong to honor him. A place where remembering the departed keeps the living alive — we hope you will be there with us.
ohYoonMemorial.printEyebrow: An era carved by the knife
ohYoonMemorial.printLead: The woodblock print Oh Yoon devoted his life to was a resolve that could not be undone once the knife touched the wood — and the most democratic art of all, one anyone could share. The faces of our time, carved by that blade.
ohYoonMemorial.printMore: See all of Oh Yoon’s works
ohYoonMemorial.printWork1Caption: In an age of censorship he slipped into the shaman’s sword dance. The people’s sorrow and defiance carved into ritual imagery — the peak of his “shamanic resistance.”
ohYoonMemorial.printWork2Caption: A laborer’s dawn, pushing back the dark to begin the day. He recorded the hours of the lowest places with the tip of his knife.
ohYoonMemorial.printWork3Caption: “No one in spring, no will in autumn.” A large composition condensing the futility of an era into a single blade.
ohYoonMemorial.printWork4Caption: A goblin in broad daylight. With the people’s ecstatic spirit — fear laced with humor — he twists an oppressive reality.
ohYoonMemorial.printWork5Caption: A singer pouring out a passage of pansori. For Oh Yoon, art was something heard before it was seen.
ohYoonMemorial.printWork6Caption: Hands gripping earth and tools. A portrait of those who held up an era from the very bottom.
ohYoonMemorial.faqQ4: Can I cancel or get a refund?
ohYoonMemorial.faqA4: Yes. Until 3 days before the event (July 2), you can refund yourself on the “Look up / cancel” page using your order number and phone number. After that, please contact the office (contact@kosmart.org). Cancellations close to the date may not be refundable due to bus and meal reservations.
ohYoonMemorial.faqQ5: Where and when does the bus leave?
ohYoonMemorial.faqA5: The bus departs at 9:30 a.m. on Sunday, July 5, next to Insadong Suun Hall. It leaves on time, so please arrive by 9:20. Staff will guide you on site.
ohYoonMemorial.faqA6: Once payment is complete, we send confirmation by KakaoTalk and email (email only if you provided one). Your phone number is used for day-of contact and guidance.
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaningTitle: The populist and artistic meaning of Oh Yoon’s work
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaning1Title: At the heart of Minjung art
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaning1Body: Joining the founding of the Reality Group (1969) and Reality and Utterance (1979), Oh Yoon pulled the reality of the lowest places into art at a time when ornate abstraction ruled the academy. With Park Bul-ddong, Min Jeong-gi, and Lim Ok-sang, he stood at the center of 1980s Minjung art.
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaning2Body: The woodblock print he chose was a vessel he could rub out by the tens of thousands with a spoon, without even a press. On poetry covers, on labor-site leaflets, in children’s storybooks — it reached the most hands, far beyond the museum.
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaning3Title: The blade’s originality — an art that listens
ohYoonMemorial.artMeaning3Body: When censorship silenced the direct voice, he entered the shaman’s sword dance and goblins, hiding resistance in ritual imagery. As in Song of the Blade and The Singer, for him art was to take down the sound of an era with the tip of a knife before it was ever seen.
